refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' (a boundary condition) and that the acceptance criterion is customer-negotiable but must be met once defined.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under simulated and live network conditions matching the agreed profile (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, latency budget, interference scenarios). Measure end-to-end packet loss for telemetry and tasking streams over a defined test duration (e.g., 1 hour continuous operation). Compare against acceptance criterion.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize effective packet loss impact on command execution.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or alert the operator when packet loss exceeds a warning threshold (e.g., 0.5%).
FR-NGHE-009.4: The network link shall be provisioned and monitored to maintain packet loss within the agreed customer target under specified operating conditions.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual-path routing) with automatic failover to reduce single-point-of-failure packet loss.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive bitrate encoding and forward error correction (FEC) to tolerate packet loss without retransmission.
DP-C: Establish a dedicated, quality-of-service (QoS) managed network link with contractual SLA guarantees and real-time packet loss monitoring.
DP-D: Combine DP-B and DP-C: use FEC at the application layer and enforce QoS at the network layer, with real-time monitoring and operator alerts.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a two-tier packet loss mitigation strategy: (1) Network tier: provision and monitor a QoS-managed network link with contractual SLA guarantees and real-time packet loss telemetry; (2) Application tier: deploy forward error correction (FEC) and adaptive bitrate encoding in the teleoperation protocol to tolerate residual packet loss, with real-time operator alerts when packet loss approaches the warning threshold.

potential_sources: DP-D couples the teleoperation system (application layer) to the network infrastructure (network layer). This is a necessary and acceptable coupling because packet loss is fundamentally a network phenomenon; mitigation requires coordinated action across both layers.

DP-D also couples the operator interface (alert mechanism) to the teleoperation control logic (FEC/bitrate adaptation). This is a weak coupling—alerts are informational and do not block command execution.
No circular dependencies detected. Network provisioning is upstream; teleoperation logic is downstream.
coupling_check.notes: The coupling is hierarchical and unidirectional (network → teleoperation). Acceptable for a safety-critical system because it enforces defense-in-depth: network SLA provides the primary guarantee; application-layer FEC provides secondary resilience.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer will define and commit to a specific packet loss acceptance criterion (e.g., ≤1%) and agree on network operating conditions (coverage, latency, interference profile).
ASSUMPTION: Network infrastructure provider can deliver and maintain the agreed QoS SLA.

RISK: If network SLA is not achievable or is violated, application-layer FEC alone may not be sufficient to meet the acceptance criterion. Mitigation: include contractual penalties and escalation procedures in the network SLA.
RISK: FEC adds computational overhead and latency. If latency budget is tight, FEC parameters must be tuned carefully. Mitigation: conduct latency sensitivity analysis during detailed design.
RISK: Operator alerts for packet loss may cause alarm fatigue if thresholds are not well-calibrated. Mitigation: tune warning thresholds based on operator feedback and safety analysis.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-D is sound but should be refined to explicitly specify: (1) the FEC algorithm and code rate (e.g., Reed-Solomon, 10% overhead); (2) the QoS parameters (e.g., priority queue, bandwidth reservation); (3) the packet loss warning threshold (e.g., 0.5%); (4) the network SLA metrics and monitoring mechanism. These details will be driven by the sub-FR decomposition and detailed design.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication stack, including: (1) command encoding/decoding and tasking protocol; (2) telemetry aggregation and encoding; (3) forward error correction (FEC) and adaptive bitrate logic; (4) packet loss detection and operator alerting; (5) integration with the Network Link interface for transmission and reception. The module does NOT own network infrastructure provisioning or SLA management (owned by Network Link); it consumes the Network Link as a service.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (e.g., movement, tool control) from the Vehicle or tele-operator console. Teleoperation module encodes these commands with FEC and transmits via Network Link.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Sends vehicle state (position, sensor readings, diagnostics) to the operator console and Safety Services. Teleoperation module encodes telemetry with FEC and transmits via Network Link. Includes packet loss metrics.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Sends alerts to Safety Services when packet loss exceeds warning threshold or when FEC cannot recover a critical command. Allows Safety Services to trigger graceful degradation (e.g., reduce speed, alert operator).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication channel to the network infrastructure. Teleoperation module transmits encoded packets and receives acknowledgments/telemetry. Network Link provides QoS guarantees and packet loss telemetry.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Teleoperation is a major subsystem but not the entire solution. Assigning IM to NGHE Solution would diffuse ownership and blur boundaries. Not recommended.

Vehicle module owns the physical equipment and its local control logic. Teleoperation communication stack is logically separate from vehicle mechanics. Assigning IM to Vehicle would couple communication concerns with vehicle hardware, reducing modularity. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module is purpose-built for remote operation. It naturally contains the FEC logic, packet loss detection, adaptive bitrate encoding, and operator alerting. All external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and align with the module's scope. Highly cohesive. Strongly recommended.

Safety Services owns safety monitoring and intervention logic (e.g., emergency stop, speed limiting). Packet loss mitigation is a communication quality concern, not a safety intervention. While Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert from Teleoperation, it should not own the FEC or bitrate adaptation logic. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural and optimal owner of DP-D (packet loss mitigation via FEC + QoS monitoring). It has high cohesion (all FEC, bitrate, and alerting logic is communication-specific), low diffusion (no leakage into vehicle or safety logic), and clear external interfaces. The module depends on Network Link (a service dependency, not a containment issue) and provides SafetyAlert to Safety Services (a clean producer-consumer relationship).
containment_risks: RISK: If the Network Link interface is not well-specified or if network SLA is not enforced, Teleoperation may be blamed for packet loss that is actually a network provisioning failure. Mitigation: establish a clear SLA contract and monitoring mechanism for Network Link; document the boundary between Teleoperation's responsibilities (FEC, alerting) and Network Link's responsibilities (QoS, provisioning).

RISK: FEC and adaptive bitrate logic may interact with vehicle control latency requirements. If latency budget is exceeded, Teleoperation may need to reduce FEC overhead or disable adaptive bitrate. Mitigation: conduct latency sensitivity analysis and define fallback modes in detailed design.

RISK: Operator alerting (SafetyAlert) may create a tight coupling between Teleoperation and Safety Services. If Safety Services changes its alert handling, Teleoperation may need to adapt. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert as a well-specified event interface with clear semantics (e.g., packet loss level, timestamp, recommended action).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: IM assignment to Teleoperation is sound. Recommended refinements: (1) Explicitly document the Network Link SLA and monitoring mechanism as a separate design artifact; (2) Define the SafetyAlert event schema and semantics in the interface specification; (3) Conduct latency sensitivity analysis to ensure FEC overhead does not violate control loop timing requirements; (4) Plan for fallback modes (e.g., reduced FEC, lower bitrate) if latency becomes critical.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (two-tier packet loss mitigation) directly and sufficiently addresses the refined FR (maintain packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold). The DP is decomposable into sub-DPs (network QoS provisioning, FEC implementation, adaptive bitrate, operator alerting) that map to the sub-FRs. No independence axiom violations detected; the DP is a coherent solution strategy.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK (LOW): FEC and bitrate adaptation logic is localized to Teleoperation module. No diffusion into Vehicle or Safety Services. Network Link is a service dependency, not a containment issue.
DIFFUSION RISK (LOW): SafetyAlert is a clean producer-consumer interface. Teleoperation produces alerts; Safety Services consumes them. No bidirectional coupling or hidden dependencies.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (0.95) and low diffusion (0.05). All DP-D elements (FEC, bitrate, monitoring, alerting) fit naturally within the module's scope. External interfaces are well-defined and minimal. Containment hypothesis is strong.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-009 is feasible via packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can expose packet loss metrics via Telemetry interface. Test harness can inject packet loss and verify FEC recovery and operator alerting. Acceptance criterion is measurable and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of DP-D to Teleoperation is clear. Teleoperation owns FEC, bitrate adaptation, and alerting. Network Link owns QoS provisioning and SLA enforcement. Safety Services owns alert handling and intervention logic. Boundaries are well-defined and non-overlapping.

recommended_revisions: Refine the Network Link SLA specification to include packet loss targets, monitoring frequency, and escalation procedures.
Define the SafetyAlert event schema (packet loss level, timestamp, recommended action) in the interface specification.
Conduct latency sensitivity analysis to ensure FEC overhead does not violate control loop timing requirements.
Plan for fallback modes (e.g., reduced FEC, lower bitrate) if latency becomes critical during detailed design.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-009 is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The refined FR identifies four sub-FRs (packet loss detection, error correction, graceful degradation, network provisioning) that must be individually designed and verified. DP-D is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation, but its implementation requires detailed design of FEC algorithms, bitrate adaptation logic, monitoring mechanisms, and operator alerting. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to drive detailed design and reduce implementation risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time (target: detection latency ≤ 100 ms, accuracy ≥ 99%).

FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement forward error correction (FEC) to recover from packet loss without retransmission (target: FEC code rate ≤ 10% overhead, recovery success ≥ 95% for single-packet loss).

FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall adapt bitrate based on network conditions to minimize latency while maintaining packet loss tolerance (target: bitrate adaptation latency ≤ 500 ms, stability ≥ 90%).

FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall alert the operator when packet loss exceeds a warning threshold and recommend graceful degradation (target: alert latency ≤ 200 ms, clarity ≥ 95% operator understanding).

FR-NGHE-009.5: The network link shall be provisioned and monitored to maintain packet loss within the agreed customer target under specified operating conditions (target: SLA compliance ≥ 99.5%, monitoring frequency ≥ 1 Hz).

assumptions: Customer will define and commit to a specific packet loss acceptance criterion (e.g., ≤1%) and agree on network operating conditions (coverage, latency, interference profile).
Network infrastructure provider can deliver and maintain the agreed QoS SLA.
Operator console and vehicle are equipped with compatible communication hardware (e.g., 4G/5G modem, antenna).
FEC and adaptive bitrate logic will not exceed the control loop latency budget (assumed ≤ 500 ms for heavy equipment teleoperation).
Safety Services will respond to SafetyAlert within a defined time window (assumed ≤ 1 second for graceful degradation).
open_risks: Network SLA may not be achievable or may be violated in practice. Mitigation: include contractual penalties and escalation procedures; conduct field trials under realistic conditions.
FEC overhead may exceed latency budget. Mitigation: conduct latency sensitivity analysis; plan for fallback modes (reduced FEC, lower bitrate).
Operator alerting may cause alarm fatigue. Mitigation: tune warning thresholds based on operator feedback; conduct human factors analysis.
Packet loss may be correlated with other network failures (e.g., link dropout). Mitigation: design for graceful degradation and emergency stop procedures.
Customer may change acceptance criterion mid-project. Mitigation: establish a change control process and document the impact on DP-D and IM allocation.
evidence_needed: Network SLA specification and monitoring mechanism (to be provided by network infrastructure team).
FEC algorithm selection and code rate analysis (to be conducted during detailed design).
Latency sensitivity analysis for control loop (to be conducted during detailed design).
Operator alerting threshold calibration and human factors study (to be conducted during design and testing).
Field trial results under realistic network conditions (to be conducted during validation phase).
SafetyAlert event schema and Safety Services integration specification (to be developed in parallel with Teleoperation detailed design).
